name: Tanqueray London Dry Gin
description: Tanqueray London Dry is the archetype of the juniper-forward London Dry style and proof that minimalism can carry a global brand. Its recipe, essentially unchanged since Charles Tanqueray settled it in the 1830s, uses just four botanicals — juniper, coriander seed, angelica root and licorice — with no citrus peel at all; the lemony brightness comes from the coriander. Bottled at a bracing 47.3% ABV in the United States, it delivers piney, peppery clarity that cuts through tonic and vermouth alike, which is why bartenders treat it as a reference gin for martinis and gin and tonics. The green shaker-shaped bottle with its red pineapple seal — a Georgian symbol of hospitality — is among the most recognized silhouettes in spirits. 'London Dry' here denotes the production style; the gin has been distilled in Scotland since the late 1990s.
history: Charles Tanqueray, a clergyman's son who chose distilling over the church, founded his Bloomsbury distillery in 1830 at about age 20 and quickly won a reputation for consistency. The company merged with Gordon's in 1898. German bombing in the Blitz of 1941 destroyed the Vine Street distillery — famously sparing a single still — and production eventually consolidated in Scotland, moving to Diageo's Cameronbridge site in Fife in 1998. Tanqueray is today one of the world's best-selling premium gins, particularly dominant in the United States.
production: A neutral grain spirit is redistilled in copper pot stills with the four botanicals steeped directly in the spirit — the classic one-shot London Dry method, with nothing added after distillation except water. Juniper dominates the bill, angelica binds and dries, coriander seed supplies citrus-spice lift and a touch of licorice root rounds the base. Distillation now takes place at Cameronbridge in Fife, Scotland, to the historic Bloomsbury recipe.

tastingNotes.nose: Piercing pine-fresh juniper, coriander citrus-spice and a clean spirity lift.
tastingNotes.palate: Dry and assertive — juniper resin, white pepper, subtle licorice sweetness and earthy angelica.
tastingNotes.finish: Crisp and long, peppery juniper with a faintly sweet rooty echo.

servingTemp: Ice-cold in a martini, or over plenty of ice with tonic and a lime wedge
facts: The recipe uses only four botanicals and no citrus peel — the lemon-lime brightness comes entirely from coriander seed.
The red seal on the bottle depicts a pineapple, an 18th-century symbol of hospitality.
The Blitz destroyed Tanqueray's London distillery in 1941; production later moved to Scotland, where the gin has been made since 1998.

timeline.event: Charles Tanqueray founds his distillery on Vine Street, Bloomsbury, London
Tanqueray merges with Gordon's to form Tanqueray Gordon & Co
The London distillery is destroyed in the Blitz; one still survives
Production consolidates at Cameronbridge, Fife, Scotland

name: Hendrick's Gin
description: Hendrick's is the gin that cracked open the modern craft-gin era: a deliberately eccentric Scottish spirit that softened juniper's grip with infusions of cucumber and Bulgarian rose, packaged in a dark Victorian apothecary bottle when every competitor was clear and tall. Created in 1999 by master distiller Lesley Gracie for William Grant & Sons, it marries spirit from two very different antique stills — a small 1860s Bennett pot still worked hard for depth, and a 1948 Carter-Head whose vapor basket lends airy delicacy — before the signature essences are added. The result is unmistakable: floral, cool and faintly sweet, with juniper present but polite. Its runaway success, driven by the now-canonical cucumber-garnished gin and tonic, licensed a generation of distillers to experiment far beyond the London Dry template. Bottled at 44% ABV in the US (41.4% elsewhere).
history: William Grant & Sons, the family firm behind Glenfiddich, bought two curious old stills at auction in 1966; decades later chairman Charles Grant Gordon asked Lesley Gracie, a chemist at the company, to develop an unconventional gin with them. Launched in 1999 and aimed first at the American market, Hendrick's grew from oddity to phenomenon through the 2000s. The name is said to honor a gardener who worked for the Grant family. In 2018 the company opened the Hendrick's Gin Palace at Girvan, a purpose-built distillery, laboratory and walled-garden complex on the Ayrshire coast.
production: Two batches are distilled from the same eleven botanicals — juniper, coriander, angelica, orris root, cubeb berries, caraway, chamomile, elderflower, yarrow, lemon and orange peel. The Bennett pot still steeps and boils them for a rich, earthy spirit; the Carter-Head passes vapor through a botanical basket for a light floral one. The two are blended, then infused post-distillation with cucumber and Bulgarian rose essences — which is why Hendrick's is a 'distilled gin' rather than a London Dry.

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh cucumber and rose petal over soft juniper, chamomile and citrus peel.
tastingNotes.palate: Cool and rounded — floral rose, melon-like cucumber, gentle juniper, coriander spice and a whisper of caraway.
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and medium-length, florals fading into soft peppery herbs.

servingTemp: Over ice with tonic and a cucumber ribbon — never lime — or in a 50/50 martini
facts: The cucumber and rose are infused after distillation, technically making Hendrick's a 'distilled gin' rather than a London Dry.
Its two stills are genuine antiques: a Bennett pot still from the 1860s and a Carter-Head from 1948, bought at auction in 1966.
The brand is reportedly named after a gardener employed by the Grant family — fitting for a gin garnished from the vegetable patch.

timeline.event: Hendrick's launched by William Grant & Sons, created by Lesley Gracie
Hendrick's Gin Palace distillery complex opens at Girvan, Ayrshire

name: Monkey 47 Schwarzwald Dry Gin
description: Monkey 47 is the maximalist masterpiece of modern gin: 47 botanicals, 47% ABV, sold in a 500 ml brown apothecary bottle with a paper band and cork. Launched in 2010 from Germany's Black Forest, it stunned skeptics who assumed so many ingredients would blur into mud — instead the gin unfolds in layers, from sharp juniper and citrus through six varieties of pepper to its signature tart lingonberries and resinous spruce. Unusually, the base spirit is distilled from molasses, and the botanicals include local oddities like blackberry leaves and acacia flowers alongside classic juniper, coriander and angelica. Soft Black Forest spring water brings it to strength. Complex enough to sip, it also makes an extravagant gin and tonic and won the IWSC's worldwide gin trophy within a year of launch, propelling German gin onto the world stage.
history: Brand lore traces the recipe to Wing Commander Montgomery 'Monty' Collins, an RAF officer in post-war Berlin who sponsored a monkey named Max at the ruined zoo, later retired to the Black Forest, and left behind a gin recipe rediscovered decades on. The documented history: former Nokia executive Alexander Stein quit telecoms in 2008, partnered with celebrated distiller Christoph Keller, and launched Monkey 47 in 2010 from a converted mill in Loßburg. Pernod Ricard took a majority stake in 2016 as the gin became a global bar-shelf fixture.
production: A molasses-based neutral spirit — rare for gin — is macerated with the softer botanicals while tougher roots and berries are vapor-infused, then everything is distilled in small copper stills. The 47 botanicals include juniper, coriander, angelica, lingonberries, spruce shoots, blackberry leaves, acacia flowers, elderflower, lavender, honey pomelo and six kinds of pepper, roughly a third sourced from the Black Forest itself. The distillate rests about three months in earthenware vessels before dilution with local spring water.

tastingNotes.nose: Bright juniper and citrus zest over pine forest, lavender, berry tartness and peppery spice.
tastingNotes.palate: Dense and layered — resinous juniper, lingonberry, bitter herbs, six-pepper warmth and a faint sweetness from the molasses base.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and evolving, pepper and pine resolving into dried berries and herbs.

servingTemp: Neat from the freezer to study it, or with a light tonic and no garnish

awards.award: Gin Trophy — International Wine & Spirit Competition

facts: The name encodes the recipe: 47 botanicals, bottled at 47% ABV.
The base spirit is distilled from molasses rather than grain — almost unheard of in premium gin.
About one third of the botanicals, including lingonberries and spruce shoots, are sourced in the Black Forest around the distillery.

timeline.event: Monkey 47 launched by Alexander Stein and distiller Christoph Keller in Loßburg
Wins the IWSC Gin Trophy, vaulting German gin to international attention
Pernod Ricard acquires a majority stake in Black Forest Distillers

name: The Botanist Islay Dry Gin
description: The Botanist is the gin of Islay — made by the Bruichladdich whisky distillery from 22 botanicals hand-foraged on the island, layered over nine classic gin botanicals. Where its Hebridean home is famous for peat smoke, this spirit captures the island's other face: machair meadows, bog and hedgerow, expressed through apple mint, meadowsweet, gorse flowers, heather, bog myrtle and wild thyme. It is distilled achingly slowly — roughly 17 hours a run — in 'Ugly Betty', a squat Lomond still rescued from the demolished Inverleven distillery, at an unhurried simmer that preserves delicate aromatics. The result is a soft, herbaceous, gently floral gin with real mid-palate texture at 46% ABV, equally suited to a meditative martini or an herb-garnished gin and tonic. The embossed bottle lists all 22 foraged botanicals in Latin.
history: When Mark Reynier's team resurrected Bruichladdich in 2001, the distillery embraced experimentation; the gin project arrived at the end of the decade as a way to use spare still capacity and celebrate Islay's flora. Botanical scientists Richard and Mavis Gulliver identified the island plants that could be sustainably picked, and veteran distiller Jim McEwan shaped the spirit, first released in 2011. Professional foragers now gather the harvest each season. Rémy Cointreau's 2012 purchase of Bruichladdich carried The Botanist with it, funding global distribution.
production: Nine core botanicals — juniper, coriander seed, angelica, cassia, cinnamon, licorice, orris root, lemon and orange peel — are macerated overnight in grain spirit in the Lomond still nicknamed Ugly Betty. The 22 foraged Islay botanicals go into a vapor basket, and distillation proceeds at a bare simmer for around 17 hours, among the slowest in gin. One-shot spirit is cut with Islay water to 46% ABV; nothing is added after distillation.

tastingNotes.nose: Meadow herbs, mint and citrus blossom over restrained juniper, with honeyed gorse and coastal freshness.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and herbaceous — sweet cicely, thyme, elderflower and lemon balm around a firm juniper-coriander core.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and gently drying, wildflowers and soft spice with a saline trace.

servingTemp: With tonic and a sprig of mint or thyme, or in a dry martini
facts: All 22 foraged botanicals grow wild on Islay and are hand-picked in season; their Latin names are embossed on the bottle.
It is distilled in 'Ugly Betty', a Lomond still salvaged from the Inverleven distillery and barged to Islay.
Each run takes about 17 hours at a bare simmer — one of the slowest distillations of any gin.

timeline.event: The Botanist launches as Islay's first dry gin
Rémy Cointreau acquires Bruichladdich Distillery, including The Botanist

name: Plymouth Gin
description: Plymouth Gin is both a brand and a style: a slightly earthier, softer, subtly sweeter counterpoint to London Dry, made continuously at the Black Friars Distillery on Plymouth's Barbican since 1793 — England's oldest working gin distillery. Seven botanicals, heavy on root ingredients like angelica and orris, give it a rounded, less pine-dominant profile that made it the gin most often specified by name in early cocktail books, including the first printed dry martini recipes. Its Royal Navy history runs deep: the fleet carried Plymouth around the world, and the distillery still makes a 57% Navy Strength. The style enjoyed its own EU geographical indication until the producers let it lapse in 2015, leaving the heritage — a single distillery, a single style, one city — intact in practice if no longer in law. Bottled at 41.2% ABV.
history: The distillery occupies a former Dominican friary — hence Black Friars — with parts of the building dating to the early 15th century; Pilgrim Fathers are said to have lodged there in 1620 before sailing on the Mayflower. Distilling under Coates & Co began in 1793, and by the late 19th century Plymouth was among the world's biggest gin names, a thousand cases a week crossing the Atlantic. After decline and a 1990s management buyout revival, the brand passed via V&S to Pernod Ricard in 2008.
production: A one-shot batch distillation in a Victorian copper pot still redistills wheat-based neutral spirit with seven botanicals: juniper, coriander seed, sweet orange peel, lemon peel, green cardamom, angelica root and orris root. The higher proportion of roots relative to juniper yields Plymouth's trademark earthy softness, and Dartmoor water brings it to 41.2%. Nothing is added post-distillation, but the style is legally distinct from London Dry by tradition and former GI.

tastingNotes.nose: Soft juniper, fresh orange and lemon peel, cardamom and a damp, rooty earthiness.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and full for the strength — earthy angelica, gentle juniper, citrus sweetness and warm spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, softly spiced, with orange and orris lingering rather than pine.

servingTemp: In a stirred, ice-cold martini or a classic gin and tonic with orange peel
facts: The Black Friars building dates to the early 1400s, and the Pilgrim Fathers reputedly spent their last night in England there before the 1620 Mayflower voyage.
Plymouth Gin held its own EU protected geographical indication until the producers chose not to renew it in 2015.
Early cocktail bibles, including some of the first dry martini recipes in print, called for Plymouth by name.

timeline.event: Coates & Co begins distilling gin at the Black Friars Distillery, Plymouth
Brand joins Pernod Ricard through the acquisition of V&S Group
EU geographical indication for Plymouth Gin allowed to lapse
